frameworks.name: incidental memory encoding
frameworks.one_line: fixations on distractors as a potential implicit memory substrate for later search

frameworks.name: attentional priority / task relevance
frameworks.one_line: encoding depends on whether the item was task-relevant at fixation

core_question: Do prior fixations on a distractor accelerate subsequent search when that distractor later becomes the target?
key_assumptions: fixations index covert/overt attentional sampling of items
useful memory representations should speed subsequent visual search
the same 72-item set is used across trials
manipulations: whether the now-target item was previously fixated as a distractor vs never fixated
role reassignment of items from distractor to target across trials
key_findings: previous fixations on a now-target item did not improve subsequent search time
searching for different targets in the same set did not facilitate search for other items (prior study replicated as premise)
interpretations: incidental exposure during distractor fixations does not build a usable memory representation
task relevance at the time of fixation gates whether exposure yields search-useful memory
